description: Debugging the classic mutable-default-argument bug. A helper meant to start a fresh list on each bare call instead accumulates across calls because the default list is created once at def time. Tests cover independent bare calls (the bug), explicit-accumulator use (must still work), and interleaving. A rewrite that regresses the explicit-list path fails. Stdlib only.
prompt:
This helper builds up a list of values. Bare calls are supposed to each return a fresh list, but in production later calls are somehow returning values from earlier calls:

```python
def collect(value, into=[]):
    into.append(value)
    return into
```

Observed:

```
>>> collect(1)
[1]
>>> collect(2)
[1, 2]   # expected [2]
```

Fix it. Intended behaviour: a call with no `into` returns a brand-new list containing only that value; a call that passes an explicit `into` list appends to and returns that same list. Keep the name and signature shape, use only the standard library, and give the final code in a single Python code block.

checker.test_code:
from solution import collect
a = collect(1)
b = collect(2)
assert a == [1], a
assert b == [2], b
c = []
assert collect(3, c) is c and c == [3], c
collect(4, c)
assert c == [3, 4], c
assert collect(9) == [9]
assert a == [1] and b == [2], (a, b)
print('OK')
